items.myth: Claude looks up answers in a private database of facts when you ask a question.
items.reality: Claude generates answers from patterns learned during training plus whatever you put in the prompt; it can invent plausible details when uncertain.
items.example: Asking for a fake paper title may yield a convincing citation that does not exist—always verify external facts.

items.myth: Each chat message permanently retrains the model weights.
items.reality: Standard inference uses fixed weights; learning from one session does not update the global model unless separate product features (e.g., memory) store user-controlled context.
items.example: Telling Claude your name once does not guarantee recall in a new account session without memory enabled.

items.myth: Bigger context always means perfect recall of every token.
items.reality: Very long contexts can still yield missed details or "lost in the middle" effects; structure prompts and chunk critical facts.
items.example: A 100-page paste may need an outline-first pass before detailed Q&A on page 87.
